metadata.title: Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics: A comprehensive synthesis

abstract: This article concludes the special issue "Learning Analytics 10 Years After: A Retrospective and Research Agenda" with a summary of the contributed studies. To assess their representativeness of the last decade's learning analytics research, a literature analysis was performed based on topic extraction. Following PRISMA guidelines, 3897 journal articles and conference papers in Learning Analytics were analyzed with Latent Dirichlet Allocation with Gibbs sampling to uncover common topics. Nine primary topics emerged: skills assessment and program evaluation; adoption of learning analytics in higher education; educational tool design and teacher support; student engagement in online courses; predictive modeling in education; technology integration in education; social learning and collaborative knowledge building; data mining in educational research; and online learning environments and student behavior. Time and publication type significantly influenced topic presence. The articles selected for this special issue spanned the most frequent publication themes, demonstrating their representativeness. Furthermore, the review underscores the importance of integrating methodology with educational theory, as highlighted in the authoritative review that opens the issue, paving the way for continued advancements.

sections.paper_id: Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
paragraphs: The special issue "Learning Analytics 10 Years After: A Retrospective and Research Agenda" reflects the significant growth of the topic over the past decade. To synthesize this body of work, we aimed to evaluate the representativeness of selected papers and provide a comprehensive overview of their contributions. This article concludes the special issue by examining these works and assessing their alignment with the broader field.

Accordingly, the remainder of this article is organized as follows: Section 2 provides an overview of the special issue. Sections 3-5 offer a detailed literature review, encompassing presentations of the theoretical and methodological frameworks (Section 3), the applied methodology (Section 4), and the findings (Section 5). Section 6 concludes with reflections on the representativeness of the special issue and the implications for future research in learning analytics.

2. Overview of the special issue "learning analytics 10 Years after: A Retrospective and Research Agenda" Sailer et al, (2024), Ninaus, Huber, Bauer, andGreiff (2024) open the special issue with "The end is the beginning is the end: The closed-loop learning analytics framework", an authoritative literature review that proposes a framework for learning analytics encompassing data collection, processing and analysis, and adaptivity and personalization. The study shows that notable advancements have been made in data acquisition and processing-particularly through multimodal data sources and AI technologies-but highlights remaining gaps in the theoretical grounding of personalized learning. It therefore advocates for the application of educational and psychological theory to help 'close the loop'. The study also discusses the potential of generative AI for advanced learner models and formative assessment; however, and consistent with other studies in the special issue (e.g., Hernández-García et al, 2024, Cuenca-Enrique, Del-Río-Carazo, & Iglesias-Pradas, 2024), the authors argue that it may require strong theoretical foundation to ensure meaningful and transparent application.

The second review, "Is learning analytics applicable and applied to education of students with intellectual/developmental disabilities? A systematic literature review" (Conde & Rodríguez-Sedano, 2024), explores how learning analytics can enhance educational processes for students with Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities (IDD). By addressing the study of the use of learning analytics with IDD students-who exhibit varied learning needs-the authors address a gap in learning analytics literature. The study conducts a systematic literature review to investigate the effectiveness of learning analytics for IDD students, focusing on personalizing and adapting learning experiences. The research shows that, while learning analytics can address areas like attention, memory, cognitive and language development, and social interactions, its application for IDD students is complex due to their heterogeneity. Therefore, the review highlights the need for tailored learning analytics approaches to support IDD students, aiming to improve their educational outcomes and reduce dropout rates. It advocates for developing specific interventions and classification systems to better understand and apply learning analytics in these contexts.

A third review, "Exploring the landscape of learning analytics privacy in fog and edge computing: A systematic literature review" does not directly address any of the topics, but instead offers a glimpse into the future of the technical infrastructure to support learning analytics. In their study, Amo-Filva et al., 2024 examine how the integration of cloud computing in education and multimodal learning analytics (MMLA) raise privacy concerns due to the necessity of using intrusive sensors and recording technologies. The authors explain how cloud computing's benefits for enhancing learning analytics can simultaneously intensify existing privacy issues while introducing new risks. To address these concerns, research advocates for a shift away from cloud computing towards localized computing solutions, such as Fog Computing and Edge Computing. By processing data closer to their source, these technologies may help reduce the risks associated with data transmission and storage in centralized cloud servers. They also enhance system efficiency and bolster data privacy and security by minimizing data travel distances and implementing advanced encryption techniques. Despite these benefits, the authors identify challenges that still need to be overcome to fully realize their potential in educational contexts, particularly in developing secure frameworks and policies that prioritize data privacy and efficient processing in learning analytics.

Three research studies explore the topic of student engagement in online courses. In "Group-level analysis of engagement poorly reflects individual students' processes: Why we need idiographic learning analytics", Saqr (2024) examines student engagement in online courses-and also predictive modelling in education-by highlighting the limitations of traditional group-level analysis and emphasizing the importance of idiographic learning analytics. The study stresses the need for personalized predictive models that account for individual students' unique engagement patterns, aligning with the goal of tailored interventions based on specific student characteristics and behaviors. Furthermore, the study's use of psychological network analysis reveals relationships between engagement indicators at the group and individual levels; these findings emphasize the significance of considering within-person processes in understanding student engagement in online learning environments. In "Cognitive ease at a cost: LLMs reduce mental effort but compromise depth in student scientific inquiry", Stadler et al, (2024), Bannert, andSailer (2024) explore the cognitive load and learning outcomes associated with using artificial intelligence, i.e., large language models (LLMs), compared to traditional search engines for information gathering during learning. Students using LLMs experienced significantly lower cognitive load. On the other hand, despite this reduction, they demonstrated lower-quality reasoning and argumentation. In other words, while LLMs can decrease the cognitive burden associated with information gathering during a learning task, they may not promote deeper engagement with content necessary for high-quality learning. Additionally, in "How the dynamics of engagement explain the momentum of achievement and the inertia of disengagement: A complex systems theory approach", López-Pernas and Saqr (2024) investigate the dynamics of student engagement in online learning across 18 consecutive courses using a person-centered approach and mixture models clustering. The study identifies three engagement states-active, average, and disengaged-based on behavioral indicators like frequency of activities and time spent online. The research aims to understand the evolution of engagement patterns over time and transitions between states, proposing a theoretical model for longitudinal engagement. Despite limitations in capturing all elements of course design and potential misclassification in clustering techniques, the study suggests that data from learning management systems (LMS) can adequately reflect student engagement trends.

While the latter research study also contributes to the field of data mining in educational research by utilizing trace log data to enhance the measurement of engagement, the most representative study of the special issue to cover this topic is "Reviewing the differences between learning analytics and educational data mining: Towards educational data science"; in this study, Cerezo et al, (2024), Lara, Azevedo, andRomero (2024) explain that learning analytics and educational data mining have evolved significantly since the early 2000s, gaining prominence due to the undeniable benefits and practical applications of big data in education. However, despite comprising two distinct research communities, the boundaries between them are often blurred, making it challenging to differentiate between the two. While focusing on analyzing educational data to improve learning outcomes, the two fields have different historical origins, methodologies, and areas of emphasis. From their review, the authors find that educational data mining is more technique-oriented, focusing on automated discovery from educational data, while learning analytics emphasizes practical applications and incorporates human judgment. Over time, the distinctions between the two have blurred, and collaboration and convergence between both fields are increasingly common. This trend is leading towards a broader, integrated research domain referred to as educational data science, which encompasses learning analytics, educational data mining, and other related disciplines. The convergence of these fields aims to address major educational challenges more effectively by leveraging a wide range of data-driven approaches and insights.

Two research studies have a major focus on predictive modelling in education. In "Exploring the relationship between LMS interactions and academic performance: A Learning Cycle approach", Hernández-García et al. ( 2024) question the ability of models in learning analytics research to predict student performance and explore their connection to online learning environments and student behavior. The study reviews existing models and proposes a new classification of student interactions based on learning cycle theories for predicting academic performance. The study shows that, although this new classification may improve predictions in online courses, it does not have predictive power in face-to-face settings. The study also discusses the impact of instructional design and course delivery mode on the usefulness of the data for predictive purposes, finding that predictions improve as the delivery mode transitions from face-to-face learning to online learning. The study stresses the importance of integrating interpretability and generalizability in predictive models, and advocates for a balanced exploration of theory-based (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) models in learning analytics research. In "The automated model of comprehension version 4.0 -Validation studies and integration of ChatGPT", Corlatescu et al, 2024, Watanabe, Ruseti, Dascalu, andMcNamara (2024) discuss the development and implementation of an automated model of comprehension (AMoC) in learning analytics. Specifically, they explore how LLMs can be used to model and improve reading comprehension in educational settings. AMoC simulates human reading comprehension and is based on the Construction-Integration model and the Landscape model, which describe the cognitive processes involved in reading. The latest version of the model, AMoC v4.0, incorporates ChatGPT to generate a knowledge graph that represents the reader's mental representation of the text. The model is validated through three studies, comparing its performance to previous versions of AMoC and to empirical findings from reading comprehension research. The results show that AMoC v4.0 generates inferences and activation scores that closely resemble human-generated inferences and align with human comprehension. The development of AMoC and its incorporation of LLMs provide insights into cognitive processes and have practical applications in education, such as personalizing learning materials and predicting learning outcomes.

While not directly addressing the topic of adoption of learning analytics in higher education-if, by adoption, we refer to the traditional view of acceptance and use of technologies and systems-, in "Using machine learning for continuous updating of meta-analysis in educational context", Chernikova et al, 2024, Stadler, Melev, andFischer (2024) provide a description of how machine learning algorithms facilitate semi-automatic selection of abstracts for meta-analysis on the effects of simulation-based learning in higher education. The main goal of the study is to further develop theory by using machine learning algorithms for systematic research in higher education and, ultimately, to inform practical decisions. The study highlights the need for interpretable, theory-informed models over purely data-driven approaches to ensure the interpretability and utility of predictions in educational contexts.

In the same vein, Stanciu and Nistor (2024) conduct one of the first studies of the relationship between university rankings and the theoretical frameworks used in doctoral capstones within the education field at those universities. The study is based on the 2023 Academic Ranking of World Universities (ARWU) for education, a dataset of 9770 doctoral capstone abstracts, and a curated list of 59 learning theories, applying machine learning, cosine similarity between documents, and analysis of variance for data analysis. The authors find a significant relationship between the management of educational and psychological research at a doctoral level and insights provided by learning analytics.

sections.header_raw: How representative are the special issue studies of the last decade's research on learning analytics?
sections.header_norm: how representative are the special issue studies of the last decade s research on learning analytics
paragraphs: The context of this special issue was a quickly developing research field driven by the technological advancements that underpin it (Conde, Hernández-García, & Chaparro-Peláez, 2021, pp. 583-586). As recently as 2013, the status of learning analytics was not yet fully established and was yet to be regarded as a consistent and solid scientific field-e.g., "Learning analytics is not so much a solid academic discipline with established methodological approaches as it is a 'jackdaw' field of enquiry, picking up 'shiny' techniques, tools and methodologies, including web analytics (the analysis of logs of activity on the web), social network analysis, predictive modeling, natural language processing, and more" (Clow, 2013, pp. 6-7). However, by 2017, handbooks were already being written on the topic (Lang, Siemens, Wise, & Gasevic, 2017). A simple search on Google Scholar for the theme "learning analytics", without any other limitations, yields 152,000 results, while searching for "systematic reviews" on "learning analytics"-using the Boolean query (("systematic review" OR "systematic literature review") AND "learning analytics")) results in 17,500 hits. The sheer number of publications underscores the rapid growth of the field.

This raises a central question about publication quality: How representative are the selected studies for the learning analytics research of the last decade? To address this question, the empirical section of this article examines the following research questions: What are the main topics addressed in peer-reviewed works, i.e., journal articles and conference papers? (RQ1) How have these topics evolved over the years and what notable trends can be identified? (RQ2) What are the differences between the topic trends in journal articles and conference papers? (RQ3) To what extent do the articles published in this special issue cover the main topics? (RQ4)

Addressing these research questions, we aim to provide a comprehensive overview of how learning analytics has evolved, focusing particularly on the trends and developments within the field. Our analysis explores the scope and trajectory of learning analytics topics in peer-reviewed literature. Specifically, we investigate the main themes addressed in journal articles and conference papers, trace the evolution of these themes over time, compare thematic trends between journals and conferences, and examine how these topics are reflected in the submissions to this special issue. This approach allows us to present an integrated view of the current landscape and ongoing shifts within learning analytics.

sections.paper_id: Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
paragraphs: Pursuing the research questions, topic modeling was applied to a corpus of academic publications relevant to learning analytics and published either as journal articles or conference papers. To this end, the PRISMA protocol (Page, McKenzie, et al., 2021;Page, Moher, et al., 2021) was adopted to guide identifying, screening, and selecting the input materials. The inclusion criteria were: peer-reviewed, journal articles or conference papers; written in English; and approaching a learning analytics-relevant topic. Conversely, the exclusion criteria were: grey literature or not peer-reviewed; reviews, including systematic reviews, and/or meta-analysis, in order not to inflate the prevalence of certain topics.

The review began by conducting a broad search for publications explicitly mentioning "learning analytics" in their abstracts or keywords, in six databases (i.e., Science Direct, Scopus, Wiley, ProQuest, Taylor and Francis, and Emerald Insight). Specifically, the search query used the term "learning analytics" in the publications' abstracts, and keywords. The abstracts were downloaded and automatically filtered, screened, and evaluated (Fig. 1 depicts the PRISMA-like procedure used in this analysis). A preliminary search yielded 10,117 raw results, which were deduplicated and automatically filtered for removal of erroneously reported misclassified content (stage 1 of PRISMA, Identification). During this first stage, 1900 duplicated articles were removed by comparing the digital object identifiers (DOI) or a combination of publication titles and ISBN or publication titles and authors' last names if the DOI was missing. Secondly, 1933 publications misclassified as journal articles or conference papers (e.g., notes, comments, reviews, editorials, etc.) were filter out using a combination of regular Boolean expressions. Additionally, two retracted articles were excluded. At the second stage (Screening), one study without an abstract was excluded. A further 2385 publications that mentioned "learning analytics" but lacking relevance to a learning analytics topic were also excluded. Ultimately, 3897 articles and conference papers were retained for the analysis.

sections.paper_id: Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
paragraphs: Topic modeling was used to automatically extract learning analytics themes from previous research and the studies in this special issue. Topic modeling belongs to the family of unsupervised machine learning methods and is classified among the latent variable models. More precisely, topic modeling using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a latent mixture model (Lang et al., 2017) and one of the most widely used and well-established methods for obtaining insights from texts (Gallagher, Reing, Kale, & VerSteeg, 2017;Kherwa & Bansal, 2018). Topic modeling techniques, including LDA, aim to identify sets of words that appear together often and likely denote topics or themes of the source documents (Lang et al., 2017). In this vein, topics can be operationalized as sets of frequency distributions of words, based on word co-occurrences (Blei, 2012).

The LDA approach was preferred for several reasons. First, topic modeling has been demonstrated to be effective for short-to-medium length texts as early as 2015-see, for example, its application for modeling journal abstracts in Anupriya and Karpagavalli (2015, pp. 1-5). Second, this study has an exploratory nature and did not start from a clear and consensually accepted range of topics; rather, it aims to uncover such relevant learning analytics topics. Third, alternatives such as non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), Top2Vec, or BERTopic were considered less suitable due to limitations that could hinder the study's goals compared to LDA.

The main analysis to identify relevant topics was topic modeling based on the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) methodology, as introduced by Blei et al., 2003, Ng, andJordan (2003). We followed the topic modeling framework proposed by Asmussen and Møller (2019), which suggested a two-tier approach: a preliminary preprocessing stage followed by the application of LDA for topic extraction. The preprocessing involved iterative text cleaning, setting LDA parameters, and cross-validation, while the second stage covered LDA-based topic extraction, topic overview and selection of relevant topics. The process concluded with construct definitions based on extant literature. Similar steps were proposed by Banks, Woznyj, Wesslen, and Ross (2018).

We preprocessed our corpus of abstracts by using only lowercase words and removing punctuation, numbering, and extra white spaces. Words were stemmed, and uninformative or common "stopwords" were excluded. The next step was to estimate the number of topics (denoted as K). For our document term matrix, we opted for a minimum document frequency threshold of five, but we did not impose upper threshold. Next, we analyzed three alternative topic models based on the three most likely candidate K-values identified in the previous step.

To select the optimal number of topics, we used metrics from Arun et al, 2010, Suresh, VeniMadhavan, and NarasimhaMurthy (2010), Cao et al., 2009, Xia, Li, Zhang, and Tang (2009), and Deveaud et al., 2014, SanJuan, and Bellot (2014), as implemented in Nikita (2020). As Weston, Shryock, Light, and Fisher (2023) point out, "topic modeling is a good tool for uncovering broad subject-matter-based themes but not subtle nuances, which will still require human coding" (p. 1). Therefore, selecting the number of topics required a qualitative analysis-see also Jacobi, Van Atteveldt, and Welbers (2016) for a discussion about granularity and choosing K-aimed at maximizing topic separation and conceptual coverage. Lastly, we analyzed topic prevalence over the years for all publications both collectively and separately-e.g., journal articles versus conference papers. We were also interested in whether the differences between the identified trends by publication type or year were significant or, conversely, attributable to chance. To this end, we planned to perform a bi-factorial multivariate ANOVA (or a nonparametric alternative) with publication type and year as factors.

To ensure transparency and reproducibility, the open-source R Software (R Core Team, 2017) and a series of dedicated R packages (Feinerer & Hornik, 2024;Feinerer et al, 2008, Hornik, & Meyer, 2008;Grün & Hornik, 2011, 2023;Haddaway et al, 2022, Page et al., 2022, Pritchard, & McGuinness, 2022;Nikita, 2020;Wickham et al., 2019) were used for all computations.

sections.paper_id: Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
paragraphs: Topic identification. Based on the convergence of the LDA metrics, values of 9, 12, 14, 20, and 25 topics were indicated as the most likely topics, based on the convergence of the metrics. Of these, the minimum of K = 9 topics was deemed adequate and chosen for the topic extraction; selecting a higher K risked over-separating topics which otherwise might overlap significantly and represent different facets of the same construct.

Topic evolution and analysis of differences. The raw data were not normally distributed, therefore an isometric log-ratio transformation (van den Boogaart, 2005; van den Boogaart, Tolosana-Delgado, & Bren, 2024) was applied. The transformed data were normally distributed, which allowed a MANOVA to indicate the existence of main effects for each of the independent variables (i.e., publication type and publication year) but no statistically significant interaction effects (Table 1). Nonparametric Kruskal-Wallis tests also showed significant effects for YEAR (χ 2 = 431.4067, Df = 108, and p < 0.001) and TYPE (χ 2 = 255.8113, Df = 9, and p < 0.001). A series of factorial (two-way) robust ANOVAs followed (Table 2) using the WSR2 R package (Mair & Wilcox, 2020), to analyze each dependent variable (i.e., the number of topics). These analyses provided further confirmation of significant main effects for publication year and type but reaffirmed the lack of interaction effects between the two factors.

Nonparametric Kruskal-Wallis tests also showed significant effects for YEAR (χ 2 = 431.4067, Df = 108, and p < 0.001) and TYPE (χ 2 = 255.8113, Df = 9, and p < 0.001). A series of factorial (two-way) robust ANOVAs followed (Table 2) using the WSR2 R package (Mair & Wilcox, 2020), to analyze each dependent variable (i.e., the number of topics). These analyses provided further confirmation of significant main effects for publication year and type but reaffirmed the lack of interaction effects between the two factors.
sections.preview_head: Topic identification. Based on the convergence of the LDA metrics, values of 9, 12, 14, 20, and 25 topics were indicated as the most likely topics, based on the convergence of the metrics. Of these, the minimum of K = 9 topics was deemed adequate and chosen for the topic extraction; selecting a higher K risked over-separating topics which otherwise might overlap significantly and represent different facets of the same construct.

sections.header_raw: Evolution of topics and notable trends
sections.header_norm: evolution of topics and notable trends
paragraphs: Pertaining to RQ2 (How did these topics evolve over the years and what notable trends can be identified?), the evolution of topics covered in learning analytics research reveals both stability and change. As shown in Figs. 2 and 3, the interest in the Adoption of learning analytics in higher education shows fluctuation without a clear upward or downward trend, which suggests consistent yet variable engagement with this topic. Data mining in educational research, however, shows a noticeable decline in journal articles since 2016, though there has been a recent resurgence-possibly driven by advancements presented in recent conferences. Educational tool design and teacher support shows a steady decreasing slope, which suggests a loss in interest on the topic.

Online learning environments and student behavior exhibit a stable trend, with only minor variations, highlighting its continued relevance in the field. This topic appears to be one of the most 'stable' topics over the years, displaying very similar trends in both journal articles and conference papers. Predictive modeling in education, on the other hand, has seen a significant increase, reflecting its current relevance as a research focus in learning analytics.

Interest in Skills assessment and program evaluation has fluctuated slightly, with a general decline in conference papers but stable trends in journal articles. Initially dominated by conference papers, this topic has converged with journal articles over time, displaying similar trends in both types of publication. Social learning and collaborative knowledge building has maintained a relatively stable presence in both publication types.

Notably, Student engagement in online courses has shown a significant upward trend, emphasizing its increasing importance. However, it remains to be seen whether this growth reflects a sustained research focus or is primarily a response to emergency remote teaching during the pandemic and the subsequent rise of online education. Technology integration in education has experienced a marked increase in conference papers in the past two years, while its presence in journal articles has remained stable at lower levels.

paragraphs: While the analysis confirms the differences between publication types over the years, to address RQ3 (What are the differences between the trends of topics in journal articles and conference papers?), a comparative examination of thematic trends reveals distinct emphases in journal articles versus conference papers. This comparison involved assessing the prevalence and focus of topics by publication type.

Topics such as data mining, educational tool design or technology integration are more prevalent in conference papers, reflecting their strong technological orientation-design and implementation of algorithms, analysis techniques, learning analytics software systems, etc. In contrast, topics such as adoption of learning analytics and student engagement are more frequently addressed in journal articles, which generally accommodate more complex research designs and theoretical grounding.

sections.paper_id: Stanciu et al. - 2025 - Decoding a decade. Trends and evolution in learning analytics A comprehensive synthesis
paragraphs: The closing study of this special issue demonstrates that the studies are highly representative of learning analytics research over the past decade. Additionally, the trend analysis supports theory development by illustrating how the academic community's interests and priorities evolve in response to technological advances and changing educational needs. Practically, understanding these trends helps educators and policymakers anticipate future directions in the field and allocate resources more effectively. The comparative analysis also highlights both similarities and differences between journal articles and conference papers. Each medium seemingly serves different purposes: journal articles tend to offer indepth analyses and rigorous methodologies, while conference papers often introduce innovative, preliminary findings and technological advancements. This differentiation contributes to the theoretical understanding of how knowledge in learning analytics is developed and disseminated. It can also assist academics and practitioners in choosing appropriate forums for presenting their work or searching for literature based on both immediate needs and long-term goals.

Finally, this study situates the different contributions to the special issue within the context of current and emerging topics in learning analytics. The special issue contributions cover a wide spectrum of the identified topics, ensuring the issue is comprehensive and reflective of current research trends within the field.

In the dynamic field of learning analytics, this synthesis underscores the rapid evolution of the discipline and its interdisciplinary nature. Over the past decade, learning analytics has matured into a distinct research domain, as evidenced by the swift increase in publications and the emergence of multiple research strands. This growth reflects both continuity and change: while certain topics show stability over time, others experience shifts in focus and uptrends in popularity. For example, the increased emphasis on predictive modeling reflects a growing interest in leveraging data-driven approaches to enhance educational outcomes. Conversely, topics like data mining in educational research have witnessed periods of decline followed by resurgence, which are indicative of changing research priorities and evolving methodological advancements.

Looking ahead, further methodological developments supporting quantitatively and qualitatively extended data collection are likely. In addition, considering Sailer et al.'s (2024) call for theory building, the field may gradually integrate into the broader learning sciences rather than remain a stand-alone research domain. This shift is already visible in recent years (Rosé, 2018)

For example, the increased emphasis on predictive modeling reflects a growing interest in leveraging data-driven approaches to enhance educational outcomes. Conversely, topics like data mining in educational research have witnessed periods of decline followed by resurgence, which are indicative of changing research priorities and evolving methodological advancements.

Looking ahead, further methodological developments supporting quantitatively and qualitatively extended data collection are likely. In addition, considering Sailer et al.'s (2024) call for theory building, the field may gradually integrate into the broader learning sciences rather than remain a stand-alone research domain. This shift is already visible in recent years (Rosé, 2018)
